abstract:
This article provides an exegetical and theological analysis of the final pericope of the Book of Sirach (51:13-30) based on its Greek version. As the conclusion to the entire work, this text serves as a vital structural bookend, mirroring the themes introduced in the prologue (1:1-10) and offering a final synthesis of the author's theology.

The pericope is divided into two distinct sections. In the first part (vv. 13-22), the sage offers a personal, "autobiographical" account of his quest for wisdom. He emphasizes that the search must begin early in life—shaping one's character before the distractions of the world can lead one astray—and must remain a lifelong endeavor that continues until death. The wisdom described here is profoundly religious: it is sought through fervent prayer, centered on the Temple, and inextricably linked to the meticulous observance of the Law. The author outlines the multi-faceted nature of this pursuit, involving spiritual purification, the path of righteousness, and a persistent "tracking" of wisdom across all spheres of existence.

In the second part (vv. 23-30), the tone shifts from personal testimony to didactic exhortation. The sage invites the "uneducated" to join his school—the "house of instruction"—positioning himself as a mediator of divine truth. He presents two complementary paths to wisdom: the first is the rigorous, individual search he himself undertook; the second is the "institutional" path, where a student acquires wisdom through the specialized formation provided by an experienced master.

A defining characteristic of this pericope is its striking optimism. In contrast to the skepticism found in the Book of Job or the pessimism of Ecclesiastes regarding the accessibility of divine knowledge, Sirach asserts that wisdom is "near" and can be found with relatively little toil by those who are truly committed. The rewards of this pursuit are manifold, described as a "good possession" that includes intellectual capability ("heart"), interior joy, and the gift of an eloquent tongue used to praise God and instruct others. Ultimately, the pericope functions as a grand recapitulation of Sirach’s major themes—cult, Law, and prophecy—confirming that while wisdom is a gift from God, it is one that opens itself to all who pursue it with an undivided heart.
